# Tako AI — AI Agent for Okta

> Tako AI is an open-source, self-hosted AI agent that lets Okta administrators query their Okta tenant using plain-English questions, replacing ad-hoc scripting and manual UI navigation.

Built by Fctr (also known as Factor Identity) — https://fctr.io. Not affiliated with Okta, Inc.

Target audience: Okta administrators, IAM managers, IT GRC teams, and audit teams.

## What is Tako AI?

Tako AI is an autonomous, harness-engineered AI agent for the Okta Identity Cloud. Admins type natural-language questions and Tako reasons over the right data source—synced SQLite, live Okta API, or special-purpose tools—to return structured, accurate answers.

Name note: "Tako" is an anagram of "Okta".

Tako is also an extensible platform, not a purpose-built Okta-only tool. The same architecture can be adapted to query other products' APIs (Google Workspace, Slack, Workday, etc.) with minimal changes.

Tako AI is part of the broader Fctr AI ecosystem, which also includes MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers for Okta. See https://fctr.io for the full product lineup.

Example queries Tako handles today:
- "Why can't this user log in?"
- "List all users not enrolled in push MFA"
- "Find all deactivated accounts created in the last 90 days"
- "Which apps does this group have access to?"
- "Show me all service accounts with no MFA"

Tako is read-only today. Modify operations with human-in-the-loop approvals are in active development.

## Architecture (v3.0-beta)

Tako uses a supervisor-led multi-agent harness that routes each turn dynamically:

- Supervisor — control plane; decides which sub-agent handles each turn
- SQL Discovery — answers from a locally-synced SQLite mirror of your Okta tenant
- API Discovery — fetches live Okta data when SQL is stale or insufficient
- Special Tools — targeted workflows: access analysis, login-risk analysis, factor enrollment checks
- Result Analysis — cross-turn artifact comparison and synthesis
- Multi-Turn Conversation — session-scoped runtime state lets follow-ups reuse prior results

Context efficiency: the multi-agent design reduces token usage by ~60% compared to a single-agent approach (70k tokens vs 300–350k), which also lowers hallucination risk near context-window limits.

## Key Capabilities

- Natural-language queries over Okta users, groups, applications, policies, MFA factors, and devices
- Local SQLite sync for speed and sovereignty — querying 10,000 users locally takes milliseconds vs minutes via API; also enables complex cross-entity queries like "find users in group A who are not in group B" that would require many chained API calls
- Sandboxed code execution — all LLM-generated code is security-validated before running
- Slack bot integration via /tako slash command (opt-in, disabled by default)
- Query history and favorites for quick reuse
- CLI for unattended runs and cron jobs
- Python script export and CSV download
- Self-healing code generation with automatic error correction and API parameter validation
- Docker-ready (AMD64 and ARM64)

## Supported AI Models (tested)

Tako's structured harness means lightweight/cheap models work well—10–50x cheaper than premium models:
- Google Gemini 2.5 Flash, 2.0 Flash
- Anthropic Claude Haiku 3.5, Sonnet 3.7
- OpenAI GPT-4o mini, GPT-4.1 mini
- Ollama (local models) — for air-gapped or privacy-first deployments

"Bring Your Own LLM" has been a core design principle since v1. Enterprises locked to a specific vendor (Microsoft, Google, Anthropic, OpenAI) or running local models are all supported.

## Design Goals

- Zero hallucination — if a user belongs to 2 groups and 5 apps, Tako returns exactly 2 groups and 5 apps
- Predictable and repeatable results — same query should return the same answer; achieved through structured system prompts and context engineering
- Provide real operational value for day-to-day Okta admin tasks

## Quick Setup

Requirements: Docker, or Python 3.11+ with an Okta API token and LLM API key.

1. Clone https://github.com/fctr-id/okta-ai-agent
2. Copy .env.example to .env — add your Okta API token and LLM credentials
3. Run: docker-compose up  (or: python main.py)
4. Open http://localhost:8000
5. Click "Sync Okta Data" to populate the local database
6. Start asking questions
